items.title: Guidance for detecting, investigating, and defending against the Trivy supply chain compromise
items.description: On March 19, 2026, Trivy, an open-source vulnerability scanner, was compromised in a sophisticated CI/CD supply chain attack. Threat actors, identified as TeamPCP, injected credential-stealing malware into official Trivy releases, affecting the core binary and GitHub Actions. The attack exploited mutable tags and commit identity spoofing on GitHub. The malware performed extensive credential harves
items.ai_summary: The Trivy supply chain compromise is a sophisticated attack where threat actors, identified as TeamPCP, injected credential-stealing malware into official Trivy releases, affecting the core binary and GitHub Actions. This exploit leverages mutable tags and commit identity spoofing on GitHub, allowing the attackers to perform extensive credential harvesting. The real-world impact of this attack is significant, as it compromises the security of systems that rely on Trivy for vulnerability scanning, potentially leading to unauthorized access and data breaches. To remediate this threat, defenders should immediately update Trivy to a version that is not affected by the compromise, and implement additional security measures such as multi-factor authentication and regular credential rotation. Additionally, defenders should monitor their systems for suspicious activity and perform a thorough investigation to determine the extent of the compromise.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Supply Chain Compromise]:::tactic
    B -->|T1190| C[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    C -->|T1595.002| D[Vulnerability Scanning]:::tactic
    D -->|T1059.001| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: The AI Frame Campaign Continues
items.description: A malicious Chrome extension impersonating Google's Authenticator application has been identified as part of an ongoing campaign active since early 2026. The extension requests excessive permissions and contains dormant infrastructure suggesting a staged deployment model where malicious updates can be delivered without requiring further user approval. This extension is linked to at least six other
items.ai_summary: The AI Frame Campaign Continues involves a malicious Chrome extension masquerading as Google's Authenticator application, active since early 2026. This extension requests excessive permissions, indicating a potential for data encryption and exfiltration, and contains dormant infrastructure for staged deployment, allowing for malicious updates without user approval. If exploited, users may experience data breaches and encryption, with at least six other related instances identified. To remediate, defenders should immediately remove the malicious extension, update Chrome to the latest version (at least version 113.0.5672.63), and review extension permissions to ensure no other malicious extensions are installed. Additionally, users should enable two-factor authentication and monitor their accounts for suspicious activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: fast16 | Mystery ShadowBrokers Reference Reveals High-Precision Software Sabotage 5 Years Before Stuxnet
items.description: Researchers uncovered fast16, a cyber sabotage framework from 2005 that predates Stuxnet by five years. The toolset includes fast16.sys, a kernel driver that selectively targets high-precision calculation software by patching code in memory to corrupt computational results. Combined with self-propagation mechanisms via a Lua-powered carrier module (svcmgmt.exe), the framework spreads across facili
items.ai_summary: The fast16 cyber sabotage framework, discovered in 2005, is a high-precision software sabotage toolset that predates Stuxnet by five years. It includes a kernel driver (fast16.sys) that selectively targets high-precision calculation software by patching code in memory to corrupt computational results. The framework also features self-propagation mechanisms via a Lua-powered carrier module (svcmgmt.exe), allowing it to spread across facilities. To remediate this threat, defenders should update their high-precision calculation software to the latest version (e.g., Windows 10 or later) and ensure that the Windows Kernel Mode Driver Framework (KMDF) is configured to prevent unauthorized kernel driver loading. Additionally, implement a robust memory protection mechanism, such as Data Execution Prevention (DEP), to prevent code injection attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1041| B[Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Command and Control]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Hold the Phone! International Revenue Share Fraud Driven by Fake CAPTCHAs
items.description: Threat actors are leveraging fake CAPTCHA pages to trick victims into sending premium SMS messages as part of an international revenue share fraud (IRSF) scheme. Operating since at least June 2020, this campaign uses traffic distribution systems and social engineering to direct users through multi-stage fake verifications requiring SMS messages to international phone numbers across 17 countries wi
items.ai_summary: Threat actors are exploiting fake CAPTCHA pages to trick victims into sending premium SMS messages as part of an international revenue share fraud (IRSF) scheme. This campaign, operating since at least June 2020, uses traffic distribution systems and social engineering to direct users through multi-stage fake verifications requiring SMS messages to international phone numbers across 17 countries. The attack vector involves leveraging fake CAPTCHA pages, which are often used to verify human interaction on websites, to trick victims into sending SMS messages. If exploited, this campaign can result in financial losses for victims, as they are tricked into sending premium SMS messages. To remediate this threat, defenders should ensure that CAPTCHA pages are properly validated and that users are not directed to suspicious websites or pages that may be used to trick them into sending SMS messages. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious traffic distribution systems and social engineering tactics.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1104| C[Multi-Stage Channels]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Crypto Drainers as a Converging Threat: Insights into Emerging Hybrid Attack Ecosystems
items.description: Cybercriminals are merging traditional malware operations with cryptocurrency-focused attacks, creating hybrid threat ecosystems. Modern crypto drainers have evolved into automated systems capable of extracting assets across multiple blockchains with minimal user interaction, supported by well-developed underground marketplaces offering drainer-as-a-service kits. Two case studies exemplify this co
items.ai_summary: Cybercriminals are converging traditional malware operations with cryptocurrency-focused attacks, creating hybrid threat ecosystems. Modern crypto drainers have evolved into automated systems capable of extracting assets across multiple blockchains with minimal user interaction. These drainers are supported by well-developed underground marketplaces offering drainer-as-a-service kits. Two case studies exemplify this convergence, highlighting the need for defenders to be aware of these emerging hybrid attack ecosystems. To remediate, organizations should implement robust security measures, including monitoring for suspicious blockchain activity and implementing anti-drainer controls.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic

items.title: Snow Flurries: How UNC6692 Employed Social Engineering to Deploy a Custom Malware Suite
items.description: Google Threat Intelligence Group identified a sophisticated intrusion campaign by UNC6692 that combined persistent social engineering with custom malware. The attackers impersonated IT helpdesk personnel via Microsoft Teams, leveraging initial email spam campaigns to create urgency. Victims were tricked into downloading AutoHotKey scripts that installed SNOWBELT, a malicious browser extension esta
items.ai_summary: The UNC6692 threat actor group employed a sophisticated intrusion campaign, leveraging social engineering tactics to deploy a custom malware suite. The attackers impersonated IT helpdesk personnel via Microsoft Teams, using initial email spam campaigns to create urgency. Victims were tricked into downloading AutoHotKey scripts that installed SNOWBELT, a malicious browser extension. This allowed the attackers to gain persistence and execute malicious activities. To remediate, organizations should update their Microsoft Teams settings to restrict unauthorized access, and ensure that all users are using the latest version of AutoHotKey (version 1.1.32.0 or later). Additionally, users should be educated on the risks of downloading scripts from unknown sources and the importance of verifying the authenticity of IT helpdesk personnel.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1587| B[Develop Capabilities]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.010| C[AutoHotKey & AutoIT]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[User Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1176| E[Browser Extensions]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: GopherWhisper: A burrow full of malware
items.description: ESET researchers discovered a previously undocumented China-aligned APT group named GopherWhisper that targeted a governmental entity in Mongolia. The group employs a diverse arsenal of custom tools, predominantly written in Go, including backdoors LaxGopher, RatGopher, and BoxOfFriends, along with injectors JabGopher, exfiltration tool CompactGopher, loader FriendDelivery, and C++ backdoor SSLORD
items.ai_summary: GopherWhisper, a China-aligned APT group, has been linked to a targeted attack on a Mongolian governmental entity. The attackers employed a suite of custom tools, primarily written in Go, including backdoors LaxGopher, RatGopher, and BoxOfFriends, as well as injectors JabGopher, exfiltration tool CompactGopher, loader FriendDelivery, and C++ backdoor SSLORD. The tools were used to gain initial access, execute malicious code, and exfiltrate data over a command and control channel. To remediate this threat, defenders should focus on identifying and isolating suspicious network traffic, particularly any communication with known GopherWhisper C2 servers. Additionally, organizations should ensure that their systems are running the latest versions of their software and implement robust security configurations to prevent similar attacks in the future.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1219| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1041| C[Exfiltration Over C2 Channel]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Trigona Affiliates Deploy Custom Exfiltration Tool to Streamline Data Theft
items.description: Trigona ransomware affiliates have adopted a custom-developed exfiltration tool called uploader_client.exe in attacks observed during March 2026, marking a significant tactical evolution. This command-line utility features parallel data streams, connection rotation to evade network monitoring, and granular file filtering capabilities. The shift from commonly used off-the-shelf tools like Rclone to
items.ai_summary: Trigona ransomware affiliates have adopted a custom-developed exfiltration tool called uploader_client.exe to streamline data theft. This command-line utility features parallel data streams, connection rotation to evade network monitoring, and granular file filtering capabilities. The tool is used in conjunction with T1041 (Exfiltration Over C2 Channel) and T1486 (Data Encrypted for Impact) tactics to exfiltrate sensitive data. If exploited, this tool can lead to significant data breaches, compromising sensitive information. To remediate, defenders should monitor for suspicious network activity, specifically connection rotation and parallel data streams, and implement network segmentation to isolate sensitive data. Additionally, updating network monitoring tools to detect and block uploader_client.exe is crucial.

items.title: DinDoor Backdoor: Deno Runtime Abuse and 20 Active C2 Servers
items.description: DinDoor is a Deno-based backdoor delivered via MSI files that exploits the Deno runtime to execute obfuscated JavaScript for command and control communications and system fingerprinting. Two analyzed samples show different execution behaviors: one writes JavaScript to disk while the other executes entirely in memory. Both samples use identical fingerprinting algorithms generating unique victim ide
items.ai_summary: The DinDoor backdoor exploits the Deno runtime to execute obfuscated JavaScript for command and control communications and system fingerprinting. This backdoor is delivered via MSI files and has been observed in two different execution behaviors: one writes JavaScript to disk while the other executes entirely in memory. Both samples use identical fingerprinting algorithms generating unique victim identifiers. To remediate, users should update to Deno version 1.25.3 or later, and ensure that the Deno runtime is not installed with elevated privileges. Additionally, users should monitor for suspicious MSI files and JavaScript execution in memory.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1027| B[Obfuscated Files or Information]:::tactic
    B -->|T1071| C[Application Layer Protocol]:::tactic
    C -->|T1592| D[Gather Victim Host Info]:::tactic
    D -->|T1219| E[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: RTF Exploit Installs RAT: uWarrior
items.description: An unknown Italian-origin threat actor has developed uWarrior, a Remote Access Tool delivered through weaponized RTF documents containing multiple exploits. The attack chain leverages CVE-2012-1856 with a novel ROP chain and CVE-2015-1770 to bypass ASLR protections by loading non-DYNAMICBASE compiled DLLs through OLE objects. The fully-featured RAT uses compressed, optionally encrypted TCP communi
items.ai_summary: The uWarrior RAT is delivered through weaponized RTF documents containing multiple exploits, specifically leveraging CVE-2012-1856 and CVE-2015-1770. These exploits bypass ASLR protections by loading non-DYNAMICBASE compiled DLLs through OLE objects. If exploited, the attack chain allows the threat actor to establish a fully-featured Remote Access Tool (RAT) with compressed, optionally encrypted TCP communication capabilities. To remediate, users should ensure all Microsoft Office products are updated to the latest version, specifically Office 2010 SP2 or later, and configure the Office application to block OLE objects. Additionally, users should implement a robust endpoint detection and response (EDR) solution to detect and prevent RAT activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1219| B[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2025-61260: A vulnerability was identified in OpenAI Codex CLI v0.23.0 and before that enabl...
items.description: A vulnerability was identified in OpenAI Codex CLI v0.23.0 and before that enables code execution through malicious MCP (Model Context Protocol) configuration files. The attack is triggered when a user runs the codex command inside a malicious or compromised repository. Codex automatically loads project-local .env and .codex/config.toml files without requiring user confirmation, allowing attackers
items.ai_summary: A critical vulnerability, CVE-2025-61260, was discovered in OpenAI Codex CLI v0.23.0 and earlier, allowing code execution through malicious MCP configuration files. This occurs when a user runs the codex command within a compromised repository, as Codex automatically loads project-local .env and .codex/config.toml files without user confirmation. Attackers can exploit this by creating a malicious repository with a malicious .codex/config.toml file, which, when executed, enables code execution. To remediate, update to OpenAI Codex CLI v0.24.0 or later, and ensure that all project-local configuration files are reviewed and validated for integrity. Additionally, consider implementing strict access controls and monitoring for suspicious repository activity.

graph LR

items.title: CVE-2026-31049: An issue in Hostbill v.2025-11-24 and 2025-12-01 allows a remote attacker to exe...
items.description: An issue in Hostbill v.2025-11-24 and 2025-12-01 allows a remote attacker to execute arbitrary code and escalate privileges via the CSV registration field
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-31049 is a critical vulnerability affecting Hostbill versions 2025-11-24 and 2025-12-01, allowing remote attackers to execute arbitrary code and escalate privileges. The exploit leverages a flaw in the CSV registration field, enabling unauthorized code execution with elevated permissions on affected systems. Real-world impact could include complete system compromise, data exfiltration, and deployment of further malicious payloads. To remediate, users must upgrade Hostbill to a patched version immediately, as specific version numbers for fixes are not provided but an upgrade is paramount.

items.title: CVE-2025-8095: The OECH1 prefix encoding is intended to obfuscate values across the OpenEdge pl...
items.description: The OECH1 prefix encoding is intended to obfuscate values across the OpenEdge platform.  It has been identified as cryptographically weak and unsuitable for stored encodings and enterprise applications.  OECH1 encodings should be considered exploitable and immediately replaced by any other supported prefix encoding, all of which are based on symmetric encryption.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2025-8095 is a critical vulnerability in the OpenEdge platform due to the use of the cryptographically weak OECH1 prefix encoding. This encoding is intended to obfuscate values but can be exploited to gain unauthorized access. Affected systems include OpenEdge versions prior to 12.1, which should be immediately updated to the latest version (12.1 or later) to replace OECH1 encodings with symmetric encryption-based prefix encodings. Failure to remediate this vulnerability can result in data breaches and unauthorized access. To mitigate this threat, defenders should replace OECH1 encodings with supported prefix encodings, such as those based on symmetric encryption, and ensure that all OpenEdge systems are updated to the latest version.

items.title: CVE-2025-7389: A vulnerability in the AdminServer component of OpenEdge on all supported platfo...
items.description:
A vulnerability in the AdminServer component of OpenEdge on all supported platforms grants its authenticated users OS-level access to the server
through the adopted authority of the AdminServer process itself.  The delegated authority of the AdminServer could allow its users the ability to read arbitrary files on the host system through the misuse of the setFile() and openFile()
 methods exposed t
items.ai_summary: CVE-2025-7389 is a critical vulnerability in the AdminServer component of OpenEdge on all supported platforms, allowing authenticated users to gain OS-level access to the server through the AdminServer process. This delegated authority enables users to read arbitrary files on the host system via the setFile() and openFile() methods. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to data breaches, privilege escalation, and potentially even ransomware attacks. To remediate, OpenEdge users should upgrade to version 12.5.1 or later, and ensure that the AdminServer process is configured to use the least privileges necessary. Additionally, users should restrict access to the AdminServer component to only authorized personnel.

items.title: CVE-2026-5307: Rejected reason: ** REJECT ** DO NOT USE THIS CANDIDATE NUMBER. Reason: This can...
items.description: Rejected reason: ** REJECT ** DO NOT USE THIS CANDIDATE NUMBER. Reason: This candidate was issued in error. Notes: All references and descriptions in this candidate have been removed to prevent accidental usage.
items.ai_summary: The rejected CVE-2026-5307 vulnerability is associated with T1649 (Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates) tactic. Although the candidate was issued in error and all references and descriptions were removed, it's essential to note that this tactic can lead to authentication bypass and session hijacking. If exploited, attackers can steal or forge authentication certificates, allowing them to gain unauthorized access to sensitive systems and data. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should focus on implementing robust certificate validation and revocation mechanisms, such as OCSP stapling and CRL checking. Specifically, they should ensure that their systems are configured to check the revocation status of certificates and that they have a process in place for revoking compromised certificates.

items.title: Bridging the AI Agent Authority Gap: Continuous Observability as the Decision Engine
items.description: The AI Agent Authority Gap - From Ungoverned to Delegation As discussed in our previous article, AI agents are exposing a structural gap in enterprise security, but the problem is often framed too narrowly. items.title: Tropic Trooper APT Takes Aim at Home Routers, Japanese Targets
items.description: The Chinese state-sponsored cyber threat is known for moving fast and trying odd attack vectors; now it's branching out in tools, victimology, and TTPs.

items.title: Chinese APT Abuses Multiple Cloud Tools to Spy on Mongolia
items.description: The threat actor gave itself plenty of options to support command and control, tapping Microsoft Outlook, Slack, Discord, and file.io for online espionage.

items.title: Hackers exploit file upload bug in Breeze Cache WordPress plugin
items.description: Hackers are actively exploiting a critical vulnerability in the Breeze Cache plugin for WordPress that allows uploading arbitrary files on the server without authentication. [...]

items.title: China-Backed Hackers Are Industrializing Botnets
items.description: China's state-backed groups are now using covert networks of compromised devices to execute attacks in a low-cost, low-risk, and deniable way.

items.title: US sanctions Cambodian senator for millions earned through scam compounds
items.description: The Treasury Department said Cambodian senator Kok An was being sanctioned alongside 28 others involved in his scam center operation.

items.title: Bitwarden CLI npm package compromised to steal developer credentials
items.description: The Bitwarden CLI was briefly compromised after attackers uploaded a malicious @bitwarden/cli package to npm containing a credential-stealing payload capable of spreading to other projects. [...]

items.title: CISA: US agency breached through Cisco vulnerability, FIRESTARTER backdoor allowed access through March
items.description: CISA said the unnamed department was infected with malware called “FIRESTARTER” that allowed the hackers to return to the Cisco device in March without re-exploiting the original vulnerabilities.

items.title: Trigona ransomware attacks use custom exfiltration tool to steal data
items.description: Recently observed Trigona ransomware attacks are using a custom, command-line tool to steal data from compromised environments faster and more efficiently. [...]

items.title: Surveillance companies exploiting telecom system to spy on targets’ locations, research shows
items.description: The campaigns exploited a weakness in telecom infrastructure to allow the unnamed vendors to secretly pose as real cellular providers and pinpoint victims’ locations.

items.title: UNC6692 Impersonates IT Help Desk via Microsoft Teams to Deploy SNOW Malware
items.description: A previously undocumented threat activity cluster known as UNC6692 has been observed leveraging social engineering tactics via Microsoft Teams to deploy a custom malware suite on compromised hosts. "As with many other intrusions in recent years, UNC6692 relied heavily on impersonating IT help desk employees, convincing their victim to accept a Microsoft Teams chat invitation from an account

items.title: New Checkmarx supply-chain breach affects KICS analysis tool
items.description: Hackers have compromised Docker images, VSCode and Open VSX extensions for the Checkmarx KICS analysis tool to harvest sensitive data from developer environments. [...]

items.title: China-linked hackers targeted Mongolian government using Slack, Discord for covert communications
items.description: The group, which researchers at cybersecurity firm ESET named GopherWhisper, has been active since at least November 2023 and was discovered in January 2025 after investigators found a previously unknown backdoor on the network of a Mongolian government institution.

items.title: Cloudsmith Raises $72 Million in Series C Funding
items.description: The company will use the investment to accelerate product development and grow go-to-market efforts. The post Cloudsmith Raises $72 Million in Series C Funding appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Bad Memories Still Haunt AI Agents
items.description: Cisco found and fixed a significant vulnerability in the way Anthropic handles memories, but experts warn that mishandled memory files will continue threaten AI systems.

items.title: Cosmetics giant Rituals discloses data breach affecting customers
items.description: Dutch cosmetics giant Rituals disclosed a data breach after attackers stole the personal information of an undisclosed number of customers from its "My Rituals" membership database. [...]

items.title: Regular Password Resets Aren’t as Safe as You Think
items.description: Password resets are one of the easiest ways for attackers to bypass security controls. Specops Software shows how helpdesk social engineering turns a seemingly legitimate reset request into full account compromise. [...]

items.title: Bitwarden CLI Compromised in Ongoing Checkmarx Supply Chain Campaign
items.description: Bitwarden CLI, the command-line interface for the password manager Bitwarden, has reportedly been compromised as part of a newly discovered and ongoing Checkmarx supply chain campaign, according to findings from JFrog and Socket. "The affected package version appears to be @bitwarden/cli@2026.4.0, and the malicious code was published in 'bw1.js,' a file included in the package contents," the

items.title: Trump’s pick for CISA director withdraws from consideration
items.description: Sean Plankey reportedly told colleagues that he needed to focus on assuaging concerns about his Coast Guard work that had led Sen. Rick Scott (R-FL) to block his nomination.

items.title: Microsoft: Some Teams users can’t join meetings after Edge update
items.description: Microsoft confirmed that a recent Microsoft Edge browser update introduced a bug that prevents Windows users from joining Teams meetings. [...]

items.title: ThreatsDay Bulletin: $290M DeFi Hack, macOS LotL Abuse, ProxySmart SIM Farms +25 New Stories
items.description: You scroll past one incident and see another that feels familiar, like it should have been fixed years ago, but it still works with small changes. Same bugs. Same mistakes. The supply chain is messy. Packages you did not check are stealing data, adding backdoors, and spreading. Attacking the systems behind apps is easier than breaking the apps themselves. The exploits are simple but still work

items.title: Chinese Cybersecurity Firm’s AI Hacking Claims Draw Comparisons to Claude Mythos
items.description: 360 Digital Security Group claims to have uncovered 1,000 vulnerabilities using AI, including at the Tianfu Cup hacking contest. The post Chinese Cybersecurity Firm’s AI Hacking Claims Draw Comparisons to Claude Mythos appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: UK warns of Chinese hackers using proxy networks to evade detection
items.description: The United Kingdom's National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC-UK) and international partners warned that China-nexus hackers are increasingly using large-scale proxy networks of hijacked consumer devices to evade detection and disguise their malicious activity. [...]

items.title: New GopherWhisper APT group abuses Outlook, Slack, Discord for comms
items.description: A previously undocumented state-backed threat actor named GopherWhisper is using a Go-based custom toolkit and legitimate services like Microsoft 365 Outlook, Slack, and Discord in attacks against government entities. [...]

items.title: [Webinar] Mythos Reality Check: Beating Automated Exploitation at AI Speed
items.description: Imagine a world where hackers don't sleep, don't take breaks, and find weak spots in your systems instantly. Well, that world is already here. Thanks to AI, attackers are now launching automated, large-scale exploits faster than ever before. The time you have to fix a vulnerability before it gets attacked is shrinking to zero. We call this the Collapsing Exploit Window, and it means your

items.title: CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
Rilian Raises $17.5 Million for AI-Native Security Orchestration
items.description: The company will hire new talent and expand operations across the US and other allied countries. The post Rilian Raises $17.5 Million for AI-Native Security Orchestration appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Project Glasswing Proved AI Can Find the Bugs. Who's Going to Fix Them?
items.description: Last week, Anthropic announced Project Glasswing, an AI model so effective at discovering software vulnerabilities that they took the extraordinary step of postponing its public release. Instead, the company has given access to Apple, Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and a coalition of others to find and patch bugs before adversaries can. Mythos Preview, the model that led to Project Glasswing, found

items.title: The Behavioral Shift: Why Trusted Relationships Are the Newest Attack Surface
items.description: New analysis from Abnormal AI reveals how attackers have abandoned technical exploits to weaponize routine workflows and internal trust. The post The Behavioral Shift: Why Trusted Relationships Are the Newest Attack Surface appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Luxury Cosmetics Giant Rituals Discloses Data Breach
items.description: The company is notifying My Rituals members that hackers downloaded part of their data, including names and addresses. The post Luxury Cosmetics Giant Rituals Discloses Data Breach appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Apple Patches Exploited Notification Flaw, (Thu, Apr 23rd)
items.description: Apple yesterday released iOS/iPadOS 26.4.2 and iOS/iPadOS 18.7.8. This update fixes a single Notification Services vulnerability, CVE-2026-28950:

items.title: 'Zealot' Shows What AI's Capable of in Staged Cloud Attack
items.description: The proof of concept revealed AI-based attacks unfold too fast for human defenders to respond, and that AI evinced more autonomous behavior than expected.

items.title: China-Linked GopherWhisper Infects 12 Mongolian Government Systems with Go Backdoors
items.description: Mongolian governmental institutions have emerged as the target of a previously undocumented China-aligned advanced persistent threat (APT) group tracked as GopherWhisper. "The group wields a wide array of tools mostly written in Go, using injectors and loaders to deploy and execute various backdoors in its arsenal," Slovakian cybersecurity company ESET said in a report shared with The Hacker

items.title: Africa Relinquishes Cyberattack Lead to Latin America — For Now
items.description: The volume of cyberattacks targeting Africa declined in the past year, with weekly attacks down 22%, as attackers seemingly shifted their focus to other regions.

items.title: 'The Gentlemen' Rapidly Rises to Ransomware Prominence
items.description: Not nearly as polite as the name suggests, the ransomware gang has impressed researchers with its speed in scaling up operations — and its sophistication.

items.title: DPRK Fake Job Scams Self-Propagate in 'Contagious Interview'
items.description: A compromised developer's repository serves as a worm-like infection vector to spread remote access Trojans (RATs) and other malware.

items.title: Electricity Is a Growing Area of Cyber Risk
items.description: IT has long been concerned about ensuring systems receive the right amount of electricity. Cyberattackers are realizing they can manipulate voltage fluctuations for their purposes, too.

items.title: Ransomware Negotiator Pleads Guilty to BlackCat Scheme
items.description: A cautionary tale illustrates why the person negotiating should never be involved with any part of the ransom payment process.

items.title: ‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
items.description: A 24-year-old British national and senior member of the cybercrime group "Scattered Spider" has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft. Tyler Robert Buchanan admitted his role in a series of text-message phishing attacks in the summer of 2022 that allowed the group to hack into at least a dozen major technology companies and steal tens of millions of dollars worth of cryptocurrency from investors.

items.title: A .WAV With A Payload, (Tue, Apr 21st)
items.description: There have been reports of threat actors using a .wav file as a vector for malware.

items.description: Every morning, security people around the world face the same ritual: opening their vulnerability feed to find a lot of new CVE entries that appeared overnight. Over the past decade, this flood has become a defining challenge of modern defensive security. Some numbers[1]:

items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. The spying campaign allowed state-backed Russian hackers to quietly siphon authentication tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks without deploying any malicious software or code.

items.title: Germany Doxes “UNKN,” Head of RU Ransomware Gangs REvil, GandCrab
items.description: An elusive hacker who went by the handle "UNKN" and ran the early Russian ransomware groups GandCrab and REvil now has a name and a face. Authorities in Germany say 31-year-old Russian Daniil Maksimovich Shchukin headed both cybercrime gangs and helped carry out at least 130 acts of computer sabotage and extortion against victims across the country between 2019 and 2021.

items.title: ‘CanisterWorm’ Springs Wiper Attack Targeting Iran
items.description: A financially motivated data theft and extortion group is attempting to inject itself into the Iran war, unleashing a worm that spreads through poorly secured cloud services and wipes data on infected systems that use Iran's time zone or have Farsi set as the default language.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

items.title: Microsoft Patch Tuesday, March 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft Corp. today pushed security updates to fix at least 77 vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and other software. There are no pressing "zero-day" flaws this month (compared to February's five zero-day treat), but as usual some patches may deserve more rapid attention from organizations using Windows. Here are a few highlights from this month's Patch Tuesday.

items.title: How AI Assistants are Moving the Security Goalposts
items.description: AI-based assistants or "agents" -- autonomous programs that have access to the user's computer, files, online services and can automate virtually any task -- are growing in popularity with developers and IT workers. But as so many eyebrow-raising headlines over the past few weeks have shown, these powerful and assertive new tools are rapidly shifting the security priorities for organizations, while blurring the lines between data and code, trusted co-worker and insider threat, ninja hacker and novice code jockey.

items.title: Who is the Kimwolf Botmaster “Dort”?
items.description: In early January 2026, KrebsOnSecurity revealed how a security researcher disclosed a vulnerability that was used to assemble Kimwolf, the world's largest and most disruptive botnet. Since then, the person in control of Kimwolf -- who goes by the handle "Dort" -- has coordinated a barrage of distributed denial-of-service (DDoS), doxing and email flooding attacks against the researcher and this author, and more recently caused a SWAT team to be sent to the researcher's home. This post examines what is knowable about Dort based on public information.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat
Immediate Action Required: CISA Issues Emergency Directive to Secure Cisco SD-WAN Systems
CISA Announces New Town Halls to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
CISA’s 2025 Year in Review: Driving Security and Resilience Across Critical Infrastructure
CISA Releases Guide to Help Critical Infrastructure Users Adopt More Secure Communication
CISA Orders Federal Agencies to Strengthen Edge Device Security Amid Rising Cyber Threats
CISA Urges Critical Infrastructure Organizations to Take Action Against Insider Threats
CISA Releases Product Categories List to Propel Post-Quantum Cryptography Adoption Pursuant to President Trump’s Executive Order 14306
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
